coredata.dc:title: Strongly emissive formamide-derived N-doped carbon dots embedded Eu(III)-based metal-organic frameworks as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of Ag+
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuanhua
Zhou, Kai
Qiu, Yan
Xia, Lan
Xia, Zhining
Zhang, Kailian
Fu, Qifeng
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of Ag+ with naked eyes was rationally designed and synthesized by encapsulating formamide-derived N-doped carbon dots (FA-CDs) in the cavities of Eu(III)-based metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs). Benefiting from the specific coordination interaction of CN groups with Ag+, the firstly synthesized FA-CDs could selectively sense Ag+ based on the turn-off mode with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.5 nM. After embedding FA-CDs in Eu-MOFs, owing to the robust self-calibration function and excellent accumulation effect of Eu-MOFs, the developed FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based ratiometric fluorescence (RF) probe has a more prominent selectivity toward Ag+ with an ultrasensitive fluorescence response across a broad range of 0.3–100 nM and an ultralow LOD of 80 pM, which is the lowest LOD value recorded of all the reported CDs-based and MOFs-based Ag+ sensors. The fabricated RF probe was successfully applied for the quantitative determination of Ag+ in environment water samples and commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution. More importantly, a novel FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF hydrogel sensor was successfully fabricated for the visual quantitative detection of Ag+ in commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution with naked eyes, which can be easily conducted without fluorophotometer-based calibration and provides an effective route to facilitate the evolution of visual fluorescent sensors from qualitative/semi-quantitative to quantitative detection.

head.ce:title: Strongly emissive formamide-derived N-doped carbon dots embedded Eu(III)-based metal-organic frameworks as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of Ag+

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of Ag+ with naked eyes was rationally designed and synthesized by encapsulating formamide-derived N-doped carbon dots (FA-CDs) in the cavities of Eu(III)-based metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs). Benefiting from the specific coordination interaction of CN groups with Ag+, the firstly synthesized FA-CDs could selectively sense Ag+ based on the turn-off mode with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.5 nM. After embedding FA-CDs in Eu-MOFs, owing to the robust self-calibration function and excellent accumulation effect of Eu-MOFs, the developed FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based ratiometric fluorescence (RF) probe has a more prominent selectivity toward Ag+ with an ultrasensitive fluorescence response across a broad range of 0.3–100 nM and an ultralow LOD of 80 pM, which is the lowest LOD value recorded of all the reported CDs-based and MOFs-based Ag+ sensors. The fabricated RF probe was successfully applied for the quantitative determination of Ag+ in environment water samples and commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution. More importantly, a novel FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF hydrogel sensor was successfully fabricated for the visual quantitative detection of Ag+ in commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution with naked eyes, which can be easily conducted without fluorophotometer-based calibration and provides an effective route to facilitate the evolution of visual fluorescent sensors from qualitative/semi-quantitative to quantitative detection.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive discharge of heavy metal ions caused by industrial expansion has seriously affected the human health and environment security [1,2]. Therefore, the sensing and detection of heavy metal ions are of great significance in many fields such as clinical diagnosis, biochemical medicine and environmental science. Among them, silver ion (Ag+) has been widely used in agriculture, medical and scientific research, which is also one of the most common pollutants in respiratory, urinary and central nervous system [3,4]. In addition, excessive absorption of Ag+ can lead to a series of toxic symptoms such as stomach pain, seizures and kidney damage [5,6]. Consequently, there is a profound significance to detect Ag+ in environmental monitoring system. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrum (ICP-MS), atomic absorption spectrum, electrochemistry and other technologies are frequently utilized for the detection of Ag+ [7–9]. However, high cost and tedious sample preparation processes greatly limit the practicality and convenience of the above detection methods. Thus, a facile, low-cost and high-performance sensing method to detect Ag+ should be developed.

In recent decades, fluorescent sensors show a great potential for the detection of various analytes due to their simple technical requirements, low cost, good portability, high sensitivity and selectivity. Luminescent lanthanide metal-organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs), which are composed of lanthanide ions and corresponding organic ligands, have attracted widespread interest in the field of chemical sensing owing to their unique fluorescent properties such as tunable emission properties, large stokes shift and long luminescence lifetimes [10–12]. Under UV excitation, their appropriate adjacent chromophores can constitute the f-f or f-d energy transfer system, which endow intense visible fluorescence emission to Ln-MOFs [13,14]. Among a wide variety of lanthanide ions, Eu(III) has attracted a great deal of attention recently because of its intense visible luminescence in the red regions [15,16]. Until now, only a few Eu-MOFs-based fluorescence sensors for detecting Ag+ have been synthesized in various ways [6,16]. However, the previous studies exhibit an unsatisfying detection limit towards Ag+ (micromolar range) and were conducted based on the monochromatic sensing mode (turn-on or turn-off), leading to poor detection accuracy due to the possible interferences from excitation intensity, sensors’ concentration and experimental medium. Alternatively, ratiometric fluorescence (RF) sensors can overcome these defects by self-calibration of dual-emission and possess a higher detection accuracy towards the targets [17–20]. Although several RF probes for sensitive and selective detection Ag+ have been reported [21,22], there is no study available to date on the high-accuracy detection of Ag+ by RF sensors based on Ln-MOFs. It is necessary to explore another guest photoluminescent (PL) nanomaterial encapsulated in Ln-MOFs to form a novel RF sensor for enhanced sensing of Ag+.

For the past decades, a series of PL nanomaterials such as quantum dots, metal nanoclusters and fluorescent organic nanoparticles have been fabricated and utilized in optical sensing [23–25]. Compared with them, carbon dots (CDs) have aroused enormous interest and been widely applied in the chemical sensing field, owing to their prominent advantages such as excellent fluorescent properties, good biocompatibility and facile preparation process [26–29]. Therefore, CDs are the suitable candidate as the PL guests encapsulated in Ln-MOFs to construct high-performance RF sensors. Although some progresses have been made in encapsulating CDs into Ln-MOFs for detecting trace analytes [30–33], most of them focused only on the use of Ln-MOFs as the internal standard of RF sensor and the potential application of CDs@MOFs for detecting Ag+ has not been studied until now. The more interesting possibility for improvement of sensitivity and selectivity caused by Ln-MOFs has seldom been unequivocally demonstrated.

Recently, the potential role of some nonluminous MOFs for enhancing sensing performance of CDs has been revealed. Chen et al. synthesized a PL-functionalized MOFs by encapsulating poly-(ethylenimine)-capped CDs into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 for enhancing sensing of Cu2+ [34]. Furthermore, a highly luminescent CDs-embedded MOFs composite material was also found to be the excellent fluorescent probe for enhanced detection of quercetin [35]. Compared with free CDs, the markedly improved selectivity and sensitivity could be mainly attributed to the excellent accumulation effect of MOFs for trace analytes which would greatly amplify the sensing signal and specificity of the guest CDs [34]. Additionally, the nanoscale cavities of MOFs could avoid the possible aggregation effect of CDs, resulting in further improvement of chemical sensing performance [36]. Thus, it is reasonable to speculate that encapsulating PL CDs into the cavities of Ln-MOFs would not only retain the self-calibration function of RF, but also contribute to enhancing the fluorescent sensing performance of the nanocomposite probes.

The excellent detection selectivity is an important precondition for the application of Ag+ sensing. Previous study showed that the presence of CN groups on the surface of CDs derived from the Schiff base reaction between CO and -NH2 groups play a crucial role in the selective sensing of Ag+, which can be ascribed to the strong coordination reaction between CN and Ag+ [37]. Interestingly, formamide (FA), which is previously utilized as the reaction solvent or regulator to assist the synthesis of CDs [37–39]. may also be a suitable precursor for fabricating CN groups functionalized PL CDs because of the coexistence of -NH2 and CO groups in its structure. However, to the best of our knowledge, there has been no study on the investigation of using formamide as the sole precursor for synthesizing fluorescent CDs.

In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent probe for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of Ag+ with naked eyes was rationally designed and synthesized by encapsulating strongly emissive formamide-derived N-doped carbon dots (FA-CDs) in the cavities of Eu-MOFs to form a novel RF probe via a facile one-pot synthetic strategy (Fig. 1 ). The design strategy of the RF sensing system could be summarized in the following three aspects. First, formamide was chosen as the sole precursor to solvothermal-assisted synthesize the guest FA-CDs because of its selective sensing performance toward Ag+. Second, Eu-MOFs were utilized as the carrier and internal standard because their robust red fluorescence in the presence of Ag+ would not interfere with the fluorescence response of blue emitting FA-CDs toward Ag+ and their Ag+ accumulation capacity contributed to the improvement of the sensing performance of FA-CDs. Third, after embedding FA-CDs in Eu-MOFs, the obtained FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs could effectively combine the advantages of the high sensitivity of FA-CDs toward Ag+, the robust self-calibration function of RF probe and the excellent signal-amplifying effect from the strong accumulation ability of Eu-MOFs for trace Ag+. Accordingly, the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF probe possesses an excellent selectivity toward Ag+ with a broad response range of 0.3–100 nM and an ultralow LOD of 80 pM, which is the lowest LOD value recorded of all the reported CDs-based and MOFs-based Ag+ sensors. Furthermore, its practicability for quantitative determination of Ag+ in environment water samples and commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution was demonstrated. More importantly, the developed RF probe was successfully applied to fabricate a novel FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF hydrogel sensor for the visual quantitative detection of Ag+ in commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution with a portable UV lamp. The RF hydrogel sensor could not only provide the yes/no Ag+ detection by the fluorescent brightness, but also accurately quantify trace concentration of Ag+ by dosage-dependent blue and red dual color responses, whose visual quantitative result was well consistent with that of ICP-MS and the nominal content of Ag+. This study not only develops a novel and facile RF-based method for ultra-sensitively detecting Ag+ and expanding the application of Ln-MOFs, but also opens an effective pathway to facilitate the evolution of visual fluorescent sensors from qualitative/semi-quantitative to quantitative detection with naked eyes.

Formamide, agarose and quinine sulfate dehydrate were obtained from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and sodium acetate were supplied by Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. Silver nitrate and other metal salts were all purchased from Kelong Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethanol, hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide were bought from RunZe Reagent Co., Ltd. Antibacterial Ag+ solution (trade name: YinErJie) was purchased from local drugstore (Luzhou, Sichuan, China). Dialysis tubes (MWCO 3500 Da) were purchased from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All the ultrapure water to prepare solutions was purified by an AWL-1002-H ultrapure water system.

A JEM-1200EX electron microscope was used to obtain transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, while a Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin electron microscope was utilized to observe high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images. Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared products were performed by a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer and Perkin-Elmer luminescence spectrometer (LS-55). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to identify the functional groups of the products by a Shimadzu IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement for elemental component analysis of the production was conducted by an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer. An Agilent 7800 ICP-MS system was used to determinate Ag+ concentration in Ag+-spiked environmental water samples and antibacterial Ag+ solution.

The synthesis of FA-CDs was conducted by a facile one-pot solvothermal method. Firstly, formamide (5 mL) and ethanol (20 mL) were mixed to form a clarified solution, which was then transferred into a Teflon-lined bomb (50 mL) and maintained at 220 ℃ for 12 h in an oven. After cooling for 4 h, the obtained black solution was dialyzed for 48 h by a dialysis tube (MWCO 3500 Da). Subsequently, the supernatant was obtained by centrifuging the dialyzed solution at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, which was filtered with a membrane filter (0.22 μm) to remove insoluble substances and then freeze-drying. Finally, the brown powder was redissolved to 1.5 mg/mL with ultrapure water and stored in refrigerator (4 ℃) for further application and characterization.

Briefly, europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O) (446 mg) and 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) (250 mg) were co-dissolved in absolute ethanol (20 mL) and then 100 mg FA-CDs powder was mixed with the solution under ultrasonic dispersion. The obtained mixture was heated for 72 h at 180 ℃ in a 50 mL Teflon-lined bomb. After centrifugation and alternately washing operation with ethanol and water to remove the unreacted precursors, the sediment was collected and dried for 24 h at 60 °C in an oven. Finally, the obtained powder was redissolved to 50 mg/mL with ultrapure water and stored in refrigerator (4 ℃) for further application. Except for the absence of FA-CDs, the unmodified Eu-MOFs were synthesized by the same methods as above.

Firstly, ultrapure water was used for diluting the Ag+ stock solution (50 mM) to series concentration of Ag+ standard solution. Subsequently, 10 μL of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension, 100 μL of series concentrations of Ag+ standard solutions and 3890 μL sodium acetate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.0) were orderly added into a 4 mL quartz cuvette. After 10 min of incubation, the emission peak intensity at 425 nm and 615 nm with different concentrations of Ag+ were recorded under 280 nm excitation. The experimental details for detecting other metal ions were same as the above operation process to confirm the selectivity of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs for sensing Ag+. All the tests were repeated 3 times and performed at room temperature (20 ℃). Except for the addition of 100 μL FA-CDs solution instead of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension, the fluorescence assays of Ag+ based on FA-CDs were conducted using the same methods as above.

The obtained FA-CDs and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs powders in above procedure were utilized as adsorbents to conduct the comparative adsorption experiments. In brief, 5 mg FA-CDs and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs powders were added into 10 mL Ag+ solutions (1 μM), respectively. After oscillation for 10 min, the supernatants were separated by centrifugation and then ICP-MS system was used to measure the amounts of residual Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Ag+ in environmental water samples and antibacterial Ag+ solution
ce:section.ce:para: Samples of river, lake and tap water were respectively collected from Tuojiang River, Yanyu Lake and our laboratory (Luzhou, Sichuan, China) to carry out the determination of Ag+ in environmental water samples. All the water samples were firstly centrifuged and then filtrated to remove insoluble impurities. The residual chlorine in tap water sample was removed by heating to boiling. The recovery rates of Ag+ based on this FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs sensing system were calculated by adding the series concentrations of Ag+ standard solutions into the various environmental water samples. The commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution was first diluted with twenty thousand times by sodium acetate buffer, then its Ag+ concentration was calculated according to the standard curve of the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF hydrogel sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the hydrogen bonding and dipole-dipole interactions between FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs and agarose, a hydrogel-based RF sensor for Ag+ detection was prepared. Firstly, agarose (0.1 g) and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension (30 μL) were added into sodium acetate buffer (10 mL) and the mixture was then heated to boiling for 3 min by an electric stove. Then the as-prepared solution was subpackaged into round molds and cooled to complete curing to obtain the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs hydrogel sheets. Afterwards, these hydrogel sheets were soaked into the sample solutions containing different concentrations of Ag+ for 10 min and the fluorescent color responses of the hydrogel-based RF sensors under a 254 nm portable UV lamp were recorded by a digital camera for follow-on visual quantitative tests. The control test of FA-CDs-based fluorescent hydrogel sensor was performed by same methods as above except for the addition of FA-CDs solution instead of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension.

The morphology and size of the fabricated FA-CDs and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs were recorded using TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 A, FA-CDs showed a good mono-dispersity without apparent aggregation, distributed from 1.5 to 5 nm with an average diameter approximately 3.5 nm (Fig. 2B). Additionally, a series of neatly arranged lattice fringes with the interplanar distance of 0.21 nm could be clearly observed on the HR-TEM image of FA-CDs (inset of Fig. 2A), which corresponded to the (100) plane lattice of graphite and thus demonstrated the existence of graphite-like crystalline structure in FA-CDs [40]. The irregular polyhedral crystals of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs with an average diameter approximately 500 nm could be clearly observed using HR-TEM (Fig. 2C). Moreover, plenty of nanoparticles were found in the HR-TEM image of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs at a higher magnification (Fig. 2D), whose shape and size were consistent with those of FA-CDs, proving the successful encapsulation of FA-CDs in Eu-MOFs. On the other hand, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of FA-CDs exhibited a broad band at 21.1° (Fig. 3 A), corresponding to the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials with abundant sp3 defects [41]. Furthermore, the diffraction peak positions of Eu-MOFs were in good accordance with those previously reported by Wang et al. [30], verifying the successful synthesis of Eu-MOFs. More intriguingly, all the primary diffraction signals related to FA-CDs and Eu-MOFs were well maintained on the XRD pattern of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs, further demonstrating that the successful doping of FA-CDs had no impact on the crystalline integrity of Eu-MOFs.

The FTIR measurements were utilized to characterize the surface functional groups of FA-CDs, original Eu-MOFs and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs. FA-CDs exhibited a broad absorption band for the stretching vibration of −OH at 3420 cm−1 (Fig. 3B). The other characteristic absorption peaks at 1637 cm-1 and 1571 cm−1 could be assigned to the -NH2 group from formamide and the CN group from Schiff base condensation reaction of formamide molecules [42], respectively. The plenty of CN groups on the FA-CDs contributed to improving their fluorescence property and might also endow FA-CDs with the Ag+-selective fluorescent response capability. The absorption bands at 3220 cm−1 of Eu-MOFs and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs were corresponding to the −OH stretching vibration of DPA, while the sharp absorption peaks at 1602 cm−1 was ascribed to the carboxyl groups of the original Eu-MOFs [43]. Furthermore, two characteristic peaks at 1571 cm−1 and 1615 cm−1 appeared simultaneously in the FTIR spectrum of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs, which could be attributed to the CN group on the FA-CDs and the amide group resulting from the dehydration condensation between the amino group of FA-CDs and the carboxyl groups of Eu-MOFs [44], indicating that FA-CDs had been firmly encapsulated into the cavity of Eu-MOFs.

The XPS spectra were applied to explore the elemental compositions of FA-CDs, Eu-MOFs and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs. The full spectrum showed that the prepared FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs were composed of C, O, N and Eu, encapsulating all the elements of FA-CDs and Eu-MOFs (Fig. 4 A). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of FA-CDs (Fig. 4B) could be fitted into three peaks at 399.5, 398.8 and 398.0 eV, corresponding to CN, CN and NH bonds [45–47]. Fig. 4C revealed that the N species of Eu-MOFs could be assigned to CN (399.5 eV) and pyridinic N (398.8 eV) bonds [48], according with the chemical structure of DPA. Furthermore, deconvolution of the N 1s signal of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs yielded three main peaks at 399.5 eV for CN bond, 398.8 eV for CN and pyridinic N bonds, 398.0 eV for NH bond (Fig. 4D), covering all forms of nitrogen on the surface of FA-CDs and Eu-MOFs. These results of XPS analysis were identical to the findings of FTIR results, verifying the successful preparation of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties, photostability and dispersity of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectrum were utilized for investigating the optical properties of FA-CDs, Eu-MOFs and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the π-π* transition at 260 nm and n-π* transition at 305 nm on the UV–vis absorption spectrum of FA-CDs might originate from their aromatic CC and CO, respectively [49,50]. The maximum emission at 425 nm of as-prepared FA-CDs could be observed under their two maximum excitation wavelengths at 280 nm and 350 nm. Accordingly, the FA-CDs exhibited a strong blue fluorescence (Fig. 5A inset) under a UV lamp (365 nm). Moreover, with the increase of excitation wavelengths, the maximum emission wavelengths were gradually red-shifted (Fig. S1A). Such excitation-dependent features of FA-CDs could be ascribed to their different sizes and heterogeneous surface states [51]. Furthermore, the quantum yield of FA-CDs was calculated to be 21 % by the reference standard of quinine sulfate. As for the unmodified Eu-MOFs, their three UV–vis absorption peaks at 214, 270 and 277 nm (Fig. S1B) were associated with n-π* transition of carboxyl groups, π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds and the pyridine ring, respectively [52,53]. Besides, Eu-MOFs had two maximum fluorescence emission at 595 nm and 615 nm under 280 nm excitation, originating from 5D0→7F1 and 5D0→7F2 electronic transition of Eu3+ [6]. Accordingly, as shown in the inset of Fig. S1, a strong red fluorescence of Eu-MOFs was observed under irradiation of 254 nm UV light. In order to make maximal use of the fluorescence properties of both FA-CDs and Eu-MOFs, the excitation wavelength of 280 nm was chosen to excite the fluorescent emission of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs. As exhibited in Fig. 5B, the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs possessed the characteristic peaks of both FA-CDs and Eu-MOFs. In particular, FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs possessed three maximum fluorescence emission at 425 nm, 595 nm and 615 nm under 280 nm excitation. Compared with the maximum fluorescence emission of FA-CDs and Eu-MOFs, there were no shifts of corresponding emission peaks in FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs, indicating that the fluorescence properties of FA-CDs and Eu-MOFs were successfully retained after the encapsulation and the composite process had no impact on their respective optical properties.

The photostability of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs is an essential premise for their possible practical applications. Therefore, the fluorescence emission in red region at 615 nm was chosen to conduct the further ratiometric applications due to its superior fluorescence stability than that at 595 nm. Furthermore, the stability of the fluorescence intensity ratio at 425 nm and 615 nm (F425/F615) under the influences of pH, concentration of NaCl and UV irradiation were further studied. As exhibited in Fig. S2A-B, the measured F425/F615 of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs excited at 280 nm basically remained unchanged as the pH value and NaCl concentration increased, demonstrating their high photostability under extreme pH and high ionic strength environments. Additionally, the value of F425/F615 still remained constant after 120 min of continuous exposure under a xenon lamp (Fig. S2C), showing their superior anti-photobleaching performance. Moreover, a satisfying dispersity and stability of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs in aqueous solution is an essential precondition for the detection of practical samples. As shown in Fig. S3A, after dispersing 10 μL of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs stock solution in 4 mL ultrapure water, no sedimentation of the obtained suspension occurred within 120 min. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color of dispersing suspension was stable and homogeneous under 254 nm UV light (Fig. S3B). From the above, the fabricated FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs possessed excellent photostability and dispersity, which were likely to be used as a novel RF sensor for chemical sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs to Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the existence of abundant CN groups on the surface of FA-CDs and inspired by previous study on the selective fluorescence quenching of CN groups functionalized PL CDs by Ag+, we speculated that the as-prepared FA-CDs might also have a selective fluorescence response to Ag+. To verify this hypothesis, the FA-CDs solutions were respectively mixed with seventeen metal ions solutions (1 μM) for evaluating their selectivity, including K+, Na+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cu2+ and Ag+. As shown in Fig. S4A, the interference for the fluorescence quenching effect of Ag+ on FA-CDs was negligible when Ag+ coexisted with most other metal ions, verifying the prepared FA-CDs had a certain selectivity for sensing Ag+. However, besides Ag+, Cu2+ could also quench the fluorescence of FA-CDs to some extent and its interference for the quenching effect of Ag+ on FA-CDs was non-negligible. By contrast, as exhibited in Fig. S4B, after mixing different metal ions solutions (1 μM) with the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension, the emission peak intensity at 615 nm remained almost unchanged, which played the role of a good internal standard. Meanwhile, the sharp decrease of fluorescence intensity at 425 nm were observed only after the addition of Ag+. When Ag+ coexisted with other metal ions, the value of F425/F615 in the presence and absence of other metal ions (including Cu2+) remained almost constant (Fig. 6 ), indicating that the interference effect of Cu2+ had been effectively excluded by the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF sensing system. This phenomenon could be ascribed to the synchronous response of fluorescence intensities at 425 nm and 615 nm induced by Cu2+. Therefore, the fabricated RF probe possessed a remarkably improved selectivity for detecting Ag+ compared with the FA-CDs-based monochromatic probe, which might be attributed to the accumulation effect of Eu-MOFs for trace Ag+ and the two-point calibration function of this RF sensing system.

The sensitivities of FA-CDs and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs toward Ag+ were also examined. The incubating time of the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based sensing system was detailedly optimized to acquire the optimum detection performance. As exhibited in Fig. S5, after the addition of 1 μM Ag+, the F425/F615 of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs was rapidly decreased till 10 min. Further extension of incubating time no longer led to the obvious decrease. Therefore, 10 min was chosen as the incubating time for the detection of Ag+ in follow-up experiments. As shown in Fig. S6A, with the increase of Ag+ concentration from 0 to 1 μM, the fluorescence intensity of FA-CDs at 425 nm decreased continuously. Furthermore, a good linear linearity (R2 = 0.9960) between the fluorescent quenching value ΔF (ΔF = F0 – F) and Ag+ concentration in range of 0.3–200 nM was shown in Fig. S6B. The LOD of FA-CDs toward Ag+ was calculated to be 0.5 nM by the formula of 3σ/slope. Afterwards, the sensitivities of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs toward Ag+ were further comparatively evaluated. The solution of FA-CDs and the suspension of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs with the same fluorescence intensity at 425 nm were respectively mixed with the same concentration of Ag+ (50 nM), and the quenched fluorescence intensity of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs was much larger than that of FA-CDs (Fig. 7 A), suggesting that FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs possessed a much higher sensitivity than FA-CDs. Moreover, as the concentration of Ag+ increased, the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs decreased gradually, while the emission peak at 615 nm remained almost unchanged (Fig. 7B). Meanwhile, the dosage-dependent blue and red dual color responses under 254 nm UV light could be clearly observed (Fig. 7B inset), which might provide a novel route to distinguish different quantities of Ag+ with naked eyes. As exhibited in Fig. 7C, the F425/F615 of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs was remarkably correlated linearly with the Ag+ concentration in the range of 0.3−100 nM (R2 = 0.9970). The LOD of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs toward Ag+ was determined as 80 pM, which was 6.25 times lower than that of FA-CDs and much lower than the tolerable concentration of Ag+ in drinking water (460 nM) stipulated by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [54].

To the best of our knowledge, the LOD of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF sensing system is the lowest value recorded of all the reported CDs-based and MOFs-based Ag+ sensors. Moreover, as listed in Table S1, compared with the recently reported RF probes for detecting Ag+, the developed FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF probe also possesses lower LOD for the detection of Ag+. The ultrasensitive fluorescence response to Ag+ can be attributed to the facts that FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs can effectively combine the advantages of the high sensitivity of FA-CDs toward Ag+, the robust self-calibration function of RF probe and the excellent signal-amplifying effect from the possible strong accumulation ability of Eu-MOFs for trace Ag+. In order to prove the capacity of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs for strongly adsorbing Ag+, the comparative adsorption studies were further performed and illustrated in Fig. S7. After adding the same amounts of adsorbents (FA-CDs and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs) into 1 μM Ag+ solution, the residual Ag+ concentrations in supernatant treated by FA-CDs and FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs were measured to be 760.8 nM and 95.7 nM, and then the Ag+ adsorption rates were calculated to be 23.9 % and 90.4 %, respectively. Therefore, the results of adsorption experiments demonstrated that FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs possessed a superior Ag+ adsorption and accumulation capacity than FA-CDs, leading to the significant improvement of Ag+ sensing performance of the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of Ag+ to FA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the quenching mechanisms of fluorescent CDs are mainly divided into four categories, including static quenching, Föster energy resonance transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect [55]. In order to distinguish the quenching mechanism of Ag+ to as-prepared FA-CDs, the possible variation of fluorescence lifetime caused by the introduction of Ag+ was measured. As exhibited in Fig. S8A, the fluorescence lifetimes of FA-CDs in the absence and presence of Ag+ remained basically unchanged and the average lifetime (τ0) was calculated to be 4.634 ns. Therefore, the possibility of dynamic quenching mechanisms, i.e. FRET and PET could be excluded [29]. Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to further verify the fluorescence quench mechanism. The relationship can be described by F0/F = 1 + Ksv [Ag+], where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of Ag+ and Ksv represents the quenching constant. As illustrated in Fig. S8B, after increasing the temperature of the solution from 20 ℃ to 70 ℃, the values of Ksv exhibited an obvious decrease from 0.0049 M−1 to 0.00321 M-1, which could be ascribed to the fluorescence recovery caused by the declining stability of the formed ground-state complex, indicating the possible occurrence of static quenching between as-prepared FA-CDs and Ag+ [55]. Afterwards, UV–vis absorption spectra were further utilized to verify the quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. S8C, after the addition of Ag+, the absorption spectrum of FA-CDs and the summed absorption spectrum of FA-CDs and Ag+ were mismatched, which might result from the combination of FA-CDs with Ag+ ions via the strong coordination reaction between the CN groups on the FA-CDs and the Ag+ ions. Collectively, the fluorescence quenching of FA-CDs caused by the addition of Ag+ could be attributed to the static quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of Ag+ in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of Ag+ in real environmental water samples, including river, lake and tap water, was performed by standard addition method to evaluate the practicability of the developed FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF sensing system. Different amounts of Ag+ (the final concentrations of Ag+ ions were 30, 50 and 80 nM) were respectively spiked into the above water samples. Subsequently, the developed RF probe and ICP-MS system were simultaneously utilized for determining the contents of Ag+ in the blank and spiked water samples. As listed in Table 1 , trace amounts of Ag+ were detected in the three unspiked water samples using both the RF probe and ICP-MS, whose values were in proximity to each other. Furthermore, the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF probe exhibited satisfying spiked recoveries ranged from 92.0 to 107.9% with the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) lower than 3.4 %, which were close to the results obtained by ICP-MS. indicating the high accuracy and practicability of the proposed method to detect Ag+. Therefore, the developed FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF sensing platform showed a high accuracy and strong practicability for the quantitative determination of Ag+ in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual quantitative detection of Ag+ in antibacterial Ag+ solution
ce:section.ce:para: Because of their powerful bactericidal action, Ag+ ions are commonly used as the main antibacterial components in commercial antibacterial Ag+ solutions. However, excessive amounts of Ag+ absorbed through the skin and mucosa might lead to various toxic effects such as seizure, stomach pain, and kidney damage. In consideration of the importance of this issue, a more facile, time-saving, and low-cost strategy for visual quantitative detection of Ag+ with naked eyes in commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution had been developed on the basis of the novel RF hydrogel sensor composed of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs and agarose. More specifically, after the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based hydrogels were soaked in Ag+ solutions of different concentrations, the blue emission of FA-CDs could be quenched in different degrees and the red fluorescence of Eu-MOFs maintained constant, leading to a macroscopic color variance of the hydrogels under a 254 nm UV lamp. In addition, the influence of incubating time on the color changes of the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF hydrogel sensor had been detailedly investigated to obtain the optimal visual sensing performance. As depicted in Fig. S9, after soaked in the solutions containing 460 nM Ag+ (maximum tolerable concentration of Ag+ in drinking water), the surrounding color of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based hydrogels turned red and gradually spread to the center within 10 min, corresponding to the penetration process of Ag+ ions into the whole hydrogel. The color hardly changed with the further extension of incubating time. Therefore, the soaking time of 10 min was selected for further studies.

Besides, the as-prepared RF hydrogel sensor was immersed in the Ag+ standard solutions of different concentrations to verify its visual sensing performance. As exhibited in Fig. 8 A, with the increasing concentrations of Ag+ ions ranged from 10 to 460 nM, the color of the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based hydrogels gradually turned from bright blue, dark blue, pale purple and purple into red, which was easy to be distinguished with naked eyes. In particular, the standard solution containing 460 nM Ag+ induced the hydrogels a significant color change from blue to red, indicating their great application potential for verifying whether the Ag+ content is within or exceed the limit in drinking water. By contrast, as shown in Fig. 8B, the FA-CDs-based fluorescent hydrogel sensor could only provide the monochromatic light response by fluorescent brightness, whose color changes were too vague to distinguish between different concentrations of Ag+. Therefore, these results clearly demonstrated that the developed FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF hydrogel sensor possessed much higher sensitivity and reliability than the contrasted FA-CDs-based monochromatic hydrogel sensor for the visual sensing of Ag+. Furthermore, the selectivity of this FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF hydrogel sensor was further studied. As exhibited in Fig. S10, after the as-prepared FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based hydrogels were immersed into various metal ions solutions with the same concentration (460 nM) and incubated for the same time (10 min), only Ag+ could completely transform the hydrogel color from blue to red, while other metal ions failed to arouse noticeable color change. The above results fully demonstrated the excellent selectivity for visual detecting Ag+ by the presented RF hydrogel sensor.

In order to visually quantify the Ag+ concentration more accurately in commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution, the color changes of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based hydrogel in the presence of different concentrations of Ag+ ranged from 50 to 100 nM had been detailedly studied (Fig. 9 ). Simultaneously, the hydrogel was also soaked into the diluted commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution for 10 min. By observation with the naked eye, the color of the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based hydrogel incubated with the diluted antibacterial Ag+ solution was apparently closer to that induced by the 90 nM Ag+ standard solution, indicating that the RF hydrogel sensor could be utilized to accurately distinguish the concentration of Ag+ in the 10 nM range by naked eyes. Furthermore, the concentration of Ag+ in the diluted commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution was determined using the FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF probe in aqueous media and the Ag+ concentration was calculated to be 97.6 nM. As a control, the corresponding quantitative result obtained by ICP-MS was 88.3 nM and the nominal content of Ag+ was around 92.7 ± 9.3 nM. Therefore, the visual quantitative result (about 90 nM) obtained by the RF hydrogel sensor was well consistent with those of FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF probe, ICP-MS and the nominal content of Ag+. Moreover, as listed in Table S2, the recently reported RF probes about the visual sensing with naked eyes could only conduct the qualitative or semi-quantitative detection toward the targets, while the developed FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF hydrogel sensor exhibited superior visual quantitative detection performance due to its strong capacity to distinguish the Ag+ concentration with high resolution. From the above, the developed FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF hydrogel sensor possesses an ultra-high sensitivity to accurately distinguish the Ag+ contents with naked eyes, which opens an effective pathway to facilitate the evolution of visual fluorescent sensors from qualitative/semi-quantitative to quantitative detection.

In summary, a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor has been rationally fabricated by encapsulating FA-CDs in the cavities of Eu-MOFs via a facile one-pot synthetic strategy for ultrasensitive and visual quantitative detection of Ag+ with naked eyes. Benefiting from the high selectivity of FA-CDs as well as the robust self-calibration function and excellent accumulation effect of Eu-MOFs, the developed FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF probe has an excellent selectivity toward Ag+ with a broad response range of 0.3–100 nM and an ultralow LOD of 80 pM, which is much lower than those of all the reported CDs-based and MOFs-based Ag+ sensors. The fabricated RF probe could be utilized to quantitatively determinate Ag+ concentration in environment water samples and commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution with satisfying recoveries and high precision. Moreover, based on the Ag+ induced dosage-dependent blue and red dual color responses, a novel FA-CDs@Eu-MOFs-based RF hydrogel sensor was successfully fabricated for the visual quantitative detection of Ag+ with naked eyes in commercial antibacterial Ag+ solution. This study not only develops a novel and facile RF-based strategy for ultra-sensitively detecting Ag+ and expanding the application potential of Ln-MOFs, but also provides an effective pathway to facilitate the evolution of visual fluorescent sensors from qualitative/semi-quantitative to quantitative detection without expensive instruments or complicated procedures.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuanhua Zhang is an MSc student in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. His major research interests include the synthesis and application of fluorescent nanomaterials and metal-organic frameworks.

Kai Zhou is an engineer in Analytical and Testing Center, Chongqing University, China. His major research interests include the development of luminescent functionalized nanomaterials and the application of biological imaging.

Yan Qiu is an MSc student in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. Her major research interests include the green synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and the preparation of non-toxic hair dye.

Lan Xia is an MSc student in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. Her major research interests include the preparation of polydopamine-derived non-toxic hair dye and synthesis of fluorescent organic nanoparticles.

Zhining Xia is a professor in School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, China. He received his PhD degree in analytical chemistry from University of Copenhagen in 1995. During 1995–1996, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Faculty of Science, the University of Copenhagen. His research is focused on the development of chromatography and spectrometry analysis technologies.

Kailian Zhang is a professor in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. She received her bachelor's degree in Pharmaceutical Chemistry in 1988 from China Pharmaceutical University and master’s degree in Chinese and Western Integrative Medicine in 2006 from Southwest Medical University. Her major research interests include pharmaceutical analysis and drug metabolism.

Qifeng Fu is an associate professor in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. He received his bachelor's degree in Applied Chemistry in 2012 and PhD degree in Chemical Engineering and Technology in 2017 from Chongqing University. His research is focused on the synthesis and application of novel fluorescent nanomaterials and the analytical methodologies on capillary electrophoresis.
